content.problemStatement:
You are given a score log of a football match between two teams. Every time when one of the teams scored a goal, the name of that team was written in the score log on a separate line.

At the end of the match, the team that scored strictly more goals than the other team wins the match. If both teams scored an equal number of goals, the match ends in a tie. Determine the name of the winning team or that the match ended in a tie.
constraints: s consists only of lowercase English letters
sum of n over all test cases ≤ 10^5
for each test case, there are at most two different team names
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing a single integer n, the number of records in the score log. Following this, there are n lines, each containing a string s denoting the name of the team that scored a goal.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one string — the name of the winning team or 'Draw' in case of a tie.

sampleTestCases.input:
2
4
ab
bc
bc
ab
3
xxx
yyy
yyy
sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, both teams 'ab' and 'bc' score 2 goals each, resulting in a draw. In the second test case, team 'yyy' scores 2 goals and team 'xxx' scores 1 goal, so 'yyy' wins.
Since there are no goals scored, the result is a draw by default.
Team 'a' scores 3 goals and team 'b' scores 2 goals, so 'a' wins.

hidden.label: general case 1
hidden.input:
1
10
a
b
a
b
a
b
a
b

hidden.label: general case 2
hidden.input:
1
10
a
a
a
a
b
b
b
b
b

solution.approach: For each test case, iterate through the score log and maintain a count of goals scored by each team. After processing all records, compare the scores to determine the winning team or if it's a tie.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the maximum number of records in a score log. This is because for each test case, we potentially iterate through all n records.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — excluding the space needed for input and output, the space used does not grow with the size of the input, as we only need to keep track of the scores of at most two teams.
